M51 is an ideal first test case due to the proximity of the system (8.58 Mpc, which corresponds to a physical scale of 41.6 pc arcsec ^ 1 ; McQuinn2016 ), large angular size on the sky ( 8 12 ), the nearly face-on orientation of M51a, the extensive multiwavelength coverage in the public archives, and the availability of many comparison results from the literature to test our procedure. In particular, the relatively well-determined timescale for the interaction between M51a and M51b ( 350--500 Myr ago; e.g., SL2000 ; Dobbs2010 ; MentuchCooper2012 ) allows us to test whether this epoch is recovered from our derived SFHs.
For M51a the majority of the IR emission is coming from the stellar populations associated with the third SFH step, with ages between 100 Myr and 1 Gyr.

sources.summary: M51a is described as the grand-design spiral in the interacting M51 pair, hosting a Compton-thick Seyfert 2 nucleus with low accretion and measurable obscuring material.

quotes.quote: M51, first cataloged by , consists of a pair of interacting galaxies: M51a (NGC 5194), a grand-design spiral galaxy, first to be classified as a spiral galaxy, and M51b (NGC 5195), a dwarf galaxy. The interaction is believed to be the cause of the distinctive spiral structure of M51a toomre72 . Indeed M51a and M51b are classed as a dual AGN: M51a hosts a Seyfert 2 in its nucleus stauffer82 that is obscured by Compton-thick (CT) material along the line of sight >1.5 10^ 24 fukazawa01 .

The AGN of M51a is found to be Compton thick, and both AGN have very low accretion rates ( _ Edd <10^ 4 ). We find that the covering factor of the obscuring material in M51a is 0.26 0.03 , consistent with the local AGN obscured fraction at 10^ 40 . The long-term 0.5--8 keV flux of the M51a nucleus remains approximately constant, varying by 20 around the mean.

sources.summary: NGC 5194 is included as a CHAOS spiral whose nearly flat abundance slope and outlying N/O behavior are linked to interaction-driven radial mixing.

quotes.quote: So far, results for individual galaxies have been reported for NGC 628 (M74) in hereafter, B15 berg15 , NGC 5194 (M51a) in hereafter, C15 croxall15 , and NGC 5457 (M101) in hereafter, C16 croxall16 . Here we present new direct abundances for NGC 3184 and, combined with past results, present the first analyses of a sample of four CHAOS galaxies, totaling 190 H 2 regions with measured auroral line based temperatures.

The four CHAOS galaxies have a range of slopes of 0.20 < _ O/H (dex /R_e ) < -0.07 . Because the high-quality direct abundances of the CHAOS sample allow us to better constrain the unique gradient of an individual galaxy, we are seeing tangible gradient differences, even amongst just 4 galaxies, but within the dispersion seen for the large CALIFA samples of strong-line abundances. In this sense, the CHAOS data are demonstrating that O/H versus R_g/R_e gradients are not uniformly behaved. NGC 5194 presents the largest deviation from the typical CHAOS slope, where its nearly flat slope has been attributed to interactions with its companion, NGC 5195, resulting in radial migration and mixing of the interstellar gas see discussion in croxall15 .

sources.summary: For NGC5194, the paper links an unusually high molecular gas fraction to tidal stirring and maps central and spiral-arm regions above the resolved main sequence.

quotes.quote: This work is based on multiwavelength observations of five nearby face-on spiral galaxies: NGC0628, NGC3184, NGC5194, NGC5457, and NGC6946.

For NGC5457 and NGC5194 the average value associated to their morphological type does not describe well their gas properties. It is well know that NGC5457 is likely to have experienced a recent event of gas accretion 2013ApJ...762...82M, 2019MNRAS.483.4968V which can explain the HI rich outer disc and its high total HI mass. A high molecular gas mass fraction, as for NGC5194, is likely the result of tidal stirring by a companion.

NGC5194 is characterised by a central region that has SFR values mostly above the MS. Positive values of _ MS are located mainly along the spiral arms, that are also well traced by an excess of total gas compared to the average. The regions A, B, C and D are all above the MS relation, but they show different properties: while the majority of pixels within C and D are have _ f_ gas,tot >0 and _ SFE,tot 0 , A and B show clumps of _ SFE,tot well above 0 and _ f_ gas,tot varying between negative and positive values.

sources.summary: The paper presents M51a as a nearby face-on grand-design spiral in a merger, with a LINER/Seyfert nucleus, roughly solar-metallicity regions, and spatially resolved PAH emission.

quotes.quote: Following the work presented in Maragkoudakis2018b , where the authors examined the PAH emission properties in resolved regions in the nearby star-forming galaxies M33 and M83, we expand the spatially resolved characterization of PAH emission in the Whirlpool galaxy (also known as M51a or NGC 5194). In addition, we examine M51a's circumnuclear region to characterize and compare the nuclear against extranuclear PAH emission.

M51a is a nearby (at a distance of 7.1 Mpc; Takats2006 ) almost face-on, grand-design spiral galaxy currently undergoing a merger with its smaller neighbor M51b (NGC 5195). Optical nuclear activity diagnostics classify the nucleus of M51a as either a Low Ionization Nuclear Emission Region Carrillo1999, Satyapal2004 or Seyfert 2 Ho1997b , with the Seyfert 2 activity potentially triggered as a result of the interaction with its companion e.g. Koulouridis2014 . The average oxygen abundance of M51a is 12 + log(O/H) = 8.54 with a -0.31 dex _ 25 ^ 1 _ 25 ^ 1 radius of the major axis at the _ B = 25 mag arcsec ^ 2 isophote deV1991rc3 . slope of the radial abundance gradient Moustakas2010 . In addition, the numerous regions present in M51a are reported to have roughly solar metallicities Bresolin2004, Moustakas2010, Croxall2015 although, the derived measurements depend on the adopted method (theoretical or empirical) used to determine the oxygen abundances.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 5194 through thousands of local star-forming sub-regions, using extinction, stellar and gas surface densities, and Monte Carlo dust coupling to estimate radiation-pressure Eddington ratios.

quotes.quote: Here we assess the role of radiation pressure in thousands of sub-regions across two local star-forming galaxies, NGC 6946 and NGC 5194. Using a model for the spectral energy distribution from stellar population synthesis and realistic dust grain scattering and absorption, we compute flux- and radiation pressure-mean opacities and population-averaged optical depth _ RP . Using Monte-Carlo calculations, we assess the momentum coupling through a dusty column to the stellar continuum.

For the individual sub-regions in NGC 5194 and 6946, we use the observed values of L_ H , the extinction A_ H , the total stellar surface density projected for each region, and the average local projected HI and molecular gas surface densities to calculate the Eddington ratio (data from Kessler2020 ). Figures figure:Edd_hist and figure:Edd_hist_planar show the distribution of Eddington ratios in sub-regions for different values of the age of the sub-regions and the height of the dusty shell. Tables table:integralQuantities and table:integralQuantitiesPlanar show the fraction of the bolometric luminosity and masses in all studied sub-regions that are super-Eddington.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5194 as a strongly interacting galaxy with unusually widespread diffuse HI, linked to its close interaction and tidal bridges and tails.

quotes.quote: The galaxies with a significant f_ diffuse ( 40 ) include NGC628, NGC 5055, NGC 5194, and NGC 5457. These four are the most face-on galaxies in the sample, with inclinations below 60 . The disk of NGC 5457 further has the largest R_ HI in our sample, while NGC 5194 also has a wide-spreading distribution due to its on-going close interaction with the companion galaxy NGC 5195.

Around the strong interacting galaxy NGC 5194, there is a significantly enhanced amount of irregularly distributed diffuse beyond the disk and tidal tails detected in dense . We study this system in detail in another project (Lin et al. in prep).
The diffuse near the tidal bridges and tails of NGC 5194, NGC 5457, and NGC 5055 may be related to the turbulent mixing.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 5194 as the main M 51 component, with a bright two-source nucleus, eight ULXs, and a reflection-dominated low-luminosity AGN spectrum.

quotes.quote: The color image shows several discrete sources, diffuse soft emission, and the bright nucleus of NGC 5194. Interestingly, the bright nucleus of NGC 5194 actually consists of two hard X-ray sources 20 apart (see below).
There are eight ULXs in addition to the LLAGN in NGC 5194.

The LLAGN of NGC 5194 The X-ray spectrum of the LLAGN is a typical of Thomson-thick Seyfert 2 galaxies, namely an extremely flat continuum, a strong and narrow line from neutral iron, and unabsorbed soft X-ray emission. Our spectral modeling shows that the flat continuum can be described as the reflection of the primary power-law ( = 1.9 ) by cold and dense material, assuming the cold material subtends a 2 solid angle to the central source, the luminosity of the primary continuum is then estimated to be 10^ 40 erg s^ 1 in the 0.3-10 band. This luminosity is about two orders of magnitude lower than that for Seyfert 1 galaxies, thus confirming the nucleus of NGC 5194 as an LLAGN as already established with previous and optical observations.

sources.summary: The paper detects polarized emission throughout NGC 5194's disk, tracing spiral structure, with low optical-disk polarization and higher fractions beyond the outer arms.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 (M51) In M51, the polarized emission is clearly detected throughout the disk, though there are large variations in the polarized fraction. The bright polarized emission traces out a spiral pattern that runs parallel to the optical arms. The minimum in polarized intensity occurs at the PA of the receding major axis (PA = 172 ^ , as tabulated in Table table:summary ). The companion, NGC 5195, is not detected in polarization. The polarization fraction at in M51 is variable, remaining lower than 5 within most of the optical disk, and increasing at large radii, beyond the outer spiral arms, to as much as 25--30 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5194 as a face-on grand-design spiral and low-luminosity starburst whose radio emission extends across the disk rather than only the nucleus.

quotes.quote: ULX 2 in NGC 5194 NGC 5194 is an almost face-on grand-design spiral galaxy with bisymmetric spiral arms of molecular gas. It is a low-luminosity starburst galaxy with faint non-thermal radio core. It is also known as the Whirlpool Nebula and it forms an interacting system with its companion NCG 5195 (Keel et al. 1985). The radio emission of this galaxy is not concentrated in the nucleus as in the previous case but it is extended over the galaxy disk. Sw04 listed a total of 7 potential ULXs for this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper applies phase-shift analysis to interacting galaxy NGC 5194, identifying corotation radii that match morphology and support intrinsic spiral modes enhanced by tidal perturbation.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 (M51) We have also used the potential-density phase shift method on the interacting galaxy NGC 5194 (M51) (Figure  ngc5194 ). By focusing on the area that just excludes the small companion NGC 5195, which is likely to lie outside of the M51 galactic plane and thus have minor influence on the internal dynamics of M51 at the epoch of observation (a conjecture which is confirmed by our analysis), the phase shift analysis gives two major CR radii (P/N crossings on the phase shift plot, represented by red circles on the overlay image) followed by two negative-to-positive (N/P) crossing radii, represented by the green circles on the image; the latter are believed to be where the inner mode decouples from the outer mode. These radii match very well the galaxy morphological features (i.e., the inner CR circle lies near the end of the bar, and the first N/P crossing circle is where the two modes are seen to decouple). For the outer mode, the CR circle seems to just bisect the regions where the star-formation clumps are either concentrated on the inner edge of the arm, or on the outer edge of the arm -- a strong indication that this second CR is located right near where the pattern speed of the wave and the angular speed of the stars match each other. This supports the hypothesis that the spiral patterns in this galaxy are intrinsic modes rather than tidal transients, and that tidal perturbation serves to enhance the prominence of the intrinsic mode, but does not alter its modal shape.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 5194 spiral-arm tracers, finding nearly coincident H I, H2, and 24 micron arm patterns and complex cross-correlations rather than systematic star-formation offsets.

quotes.quote: As an example, Figure m51 shows the H 1 emission (bottom panel) and H _ 2 map (upper panel) in polar coordinates for NGC 5194 with the contours of 24 m emission overlaid. One notes that in both cases the arm patterns in these tracers are nearly coincident. Thus, any offsets between these tracers must be small.

Figure singlecc shows a sample cross-correlation function between H 1 and 24 m at a radial annulus of 80 '' for NGC 5194. One notes two broad peaks due to the two spiral arms and the self-similarity of the structure.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC5194 as a grand-design spiral with bimodal corotation estimates and metallicity-distribution breaks coincident with literature corotation radii.

quotes.quote: NGC5194 (M51) : Grand-design spiral galaxy with 16 studies relative to its corotation radius. The values are bimodally distributed around 5.5 and 11 kpc (near the outer limit of the spiral structure). There is a very precise study on the metallicity distribution by Bresolin04 , using temperature-sensitive line methods in 10 regions and also an extensive study by , using the KK04 and PT05 methods in 32 regions compiled in the literature.

In spite of the small amount of regions in the first case, breaks in the metallicity distribution were found using polynomials of order 3 and 4, with values that are coincident with the median corotation radii from the literature. Breaks at the same position were found in the second case using fits of 3rd order polynomial, two connected lines and two straight lines with a step.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5194 as M51a, the grand-design spiral whose outer disk likely supplied the Northwest Plume and Southeast H 1 tail material during interaction with M51b.

quotes.quote: The galaxy pair M51 is one of the best known nearby interacting systems, comprised of the grand-design spiral NGC 5194 (M51a) and its early-type SB0 companion NGC 5195 (M51b).

We also trace the extremely diffuse Northwest Plume out to a total extent of 20 \ (43 kpc) from NGC 5194 (M51a), and show it to be physically distinct from the overlapping bright tidal streams from M51b. The Northwest Plume's morphology and red color ( =0.8 ) instead argue that it originated from tidal stripping of M51a's extreme outer disk.

Regarding the Southeast H 1 tail, the lack of an old, co-spatial stellar component may argue that the gas came from an extended gas-rich disk belonging to M51a. Simulations of the M51 system favor a highly inclined encounter to explain the kinematics of the tail rots90, salo00 ; such a configuration would suppress caustic formation in the tail and explain its paucity of star formation and young stars.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5194 as M 51, interacting with NGC 5195, with nuclear outflow emission, dusty spiral structure, hard X-ray nucleus, and Compton-thick classification.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 NGC 5194, also known as M 51, is interacting with NGC 5195. Optical and radio observations show extended emissions to the north and south of the nucleus, resulting from outflows generated by the nuclear activity ford1985 . The extended emission can be observed at soft X-ray energies (top-left image in Appendix multiimages ). Moreover, the HST image shows a dusty nuclear spiral structure that can also be observed at IR frequencies (see Appendix multiimages ). This galaxy was optically classified as a type 2 Seyfert see their optical spectra in Appendix multiimages ho1997 .

sources.summary: The paper compares NGC 5194 with NGC 7793, citing its LEGUS and PAWS GMC catalogs, older cluster population, and longer GMC association timescales.

quotes.quote: The Whirlpool Galaxy NGC 5194 In grasha18 we performed a similar study to constrain the timescales of the association of GMCs and star clusters in NGC 5194 (7.7 Mpc) with the LEGUS cluster catalog ( 1300 star clusters) and the GMC catalog ( 1300 GMCs) at 40 pc resolution colombo14a in the inner 6 9 kpc of the galaxy with the Plateau de Bure Interferometer Arcsecond Whirlpool Survey PAWS; schinnerer13,pety13 dataset. The median age of the star clusters in NGC 5194 is 30 Myr, five times older than the median population in NGC 7793. The increase in the total number of clusters by 4.7 times allows for a clearer trend and a stronger constraint on the disassociation timescale in a different galactic environment. In NGC 5194, the clusters located 1 R_ GMC exhibit median ages of 4 Myr, rising to 6 and 15 Myr when located in annuli between 1--2 and 2--3 R_ GMC , respectively. Star clusters unassociated with any GMC ( > 3 R_ GMC ) exhibit ages of 40 Myr, 10 Myr older than the global median.

sources.summary: The paper says radiative-transfer modeling of face-on spiral M 51a found old stars provide up to 35% of diffuse-dust heating, while M 51b affects its SED and heating.

quotes.quote: In a recent series of radiative transfer studies, developed a method to quantify the dust heating fraction related to the star-formation activity in face-on spiral galaxies, by constructing the RT model of M 51a (Paper I). The authors showcased the importance of the older stellar population ( 10 Gyr) to the heating of the diffuse dust in the ISM, donating up to 35 of its total energy budget to that cause.

The most recent RT study by investigated how the companion galaxy (M 51b) of the M 51 interacting system affects the SED, and consequently the dust heating of the main galaxy (M 51a). For that reason, a 3D RT model of the system was constructed.

The authors found that for the combined M 51 system, on average, 5.8 of the dust emission is attributed to the dust heating by the old stellar population of M 51b, of which 4.8 occurs in the M 51a subsystem; 23 of the dust heating is supplied by the old stellar population of M 51a, while the remaining 71.2 arises from the heating by the young stellar populations.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5194 as a nearby SABb spiral whose bright disk ends abruptly and whose H I velocity structure makes its geometry uncertain.

quotes.quote: The nearby galaxy NGC 5194 (M 51a, the Whirlpool Galaxy) is a SABb spiral galaxy (morphological type code T = 4.0 0.3). The bright disk of NGC 5194 ends abruptly at about 5 arcmin radius in both the optical images and the H i . The velocity structure of the gas in NGC 5194 is extremely complicated and difficult to interpret Rots1990 . There is the misalignment of the major axes of the H i distribution and the velocity field. Therefore the geometrical parameters of NGC 5194 are rather uncertain.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5194/M51a as the grand-design spiral companion in M51; its spiral arm enters the NGC 5195 field, with gas separated by a flux gap and HII/composite-line diagnostics.

quotes.quote: The interacting system Messier 51 (M51) contains a grand design spiral galaxy NGC 5194 (M51a) and an early-type galaxy NGC 5195 (M51b).

In all these flux maps, a spiral arm from the NGC 5194 connecting with region C can be seen in the southeastern region of the NGC 5195's nucleus. A low flux gap can be seen in the bottom of the FoV and under region D in all flux maps, which is suggestive that the gap separates the ionized gas emission from NGC 5195 (above the gap) and NGC 5194 (under the gap).

The spiral arm under region C, which is from NGC 5194, is dominated by regions classified as HII and composite. Considering the bottom of the field is dominated by composite regions and the lower part of the FoV is located at a spiral arm of NGC 5194 (Figure fig:df2_spec ), most of the ionized gas here should be from the NGC 5194, which is consistent with our initial suggestion in Section subsec:flux-vel-maps .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5194 as a face-on grand-design spiral interacting with M51b, with star formation in its center and arms and high CO contamination in the SCUBA-2 band.

quotes.quote: For the galaxies in question, the net contribution of ^ 12 CO to the SCUBA-2 850 m flux is typically 20 , but reaches 38 in NGC 3034 and 50 in NGC 5194, although our data reduction strategy in NGC 5194 may be suboptimal, as discussed in Section sec:ngc5194 , below .

NGC 5194 NGC 5194 (Messier 51a) is a face-on two-arm grand-design spiral galaxy, interacting with its neighbour, M51b. Both are shown in Figure fig:ngc5194 . Star formation in NGC 5194 is mostly taking place in the galactic centre and spiral arms schinnerer2017 . CO observations of NGC 5194 show chains of GMCs emerging from the spiral arms into the interarm regions koda2009, schinnerer2017 , but these features are not resolved in our observations.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 5194 using a central emission plateau, treats the Seyfert nucleus as diluted, and applies extinction corrections across the region.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 - We considered the central emission plateau of diameter 90^ . The motivation for this choice instead of selecting a smaller region around the nucleus was that the plateau represents a transition between disks and more active circumnuclear regions in terms of MIR properties. Besides, the emission from the Seyfert nucleus is diluted and completely negligible within this large aperture. We adopted the average extinction, weighted by intrinsic H luminosities, derived by Sco01 from H /Pa decrements of a large sample of H ii regions, assuming it is representative of the effective extinction throughout the central plateau. We applied a small correction to this extinction to account for the different line emissivities and extinction laws adopted.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 5194/95 as an ambiguous Whirlpool modeling case where one- and two-passage models have tradeoffs and a pre-existing warp could explain tail velocities.

quotes.quote: One example may be the ``Whirlpool'' galaxy, NGC 5194/95; after three and a half decades of modeling based on ever-better H I data there is still no consensus regarding the number of passages required to account for NGC 5194's optical morphology and extended H I tail e.g. and references therein DMFJC03 . Models in which NGC 5195 makes only one passage can account for many of the observations but have trouble matching the tail velocities, while models in which this galaxy makes two passages match the velocities better but yield double tails see Fig. 4 SL00 . One possible solution to this puzzle is to assume that NGC 5194's H I disk was warped even before its first and only encounter with NGC 5195; this could tilt the tail with respect to the inner disk in such a way as to match the observed velocities. Of course, pre-existing warps vastly complicate questions of uniquenessIn the specific case of NGC 5194/95, stronger evidence in favor of a single passage seems necessary to justify introducing such a warp; better data on stellar velocities may help DMFJC03 .

sources.summary: The paper measures morphology parameters for NGC 5194/M51 and contrasts its interacting state with isolated NGC 3184, noting changes in concentration, asymmetry, Gini, and H I interaction signal.

quotes.quote: We applied these contours on images of two galaxies, NGC 3184 and NGC 5194, spanning UV, optical, near and far-infrared from GALEX, SDSS and Spitzer see also in preparation Holwerda10a .

In Figure f:morph we can also compare the isolated, unperturbed galaxy NGC 3184 to the interacting NGC 5194 (M51). In the morphological parameters we can see that Concentration increases in the optical and near-infrared from isolated to interacting. Asymmetry decreases a little from isolated to interaction in UV and 24 m emission (the star-formation tracers) but increases for \ 21 cm emission.

sources.summary: The paper cites NGC 5194 as a disk-field example, compares foreground Faraday-depth estimates for it, and reports faint secondary polarized components offset from the bright emission.

quotes.quote: Classic examples of disk-dominated fields can be seen in galaxies such as NGC 628, 5194, and 6946, although they are present in almost all of our targets with varying detectability.
A similar consideration applies to NGC 5194, for which horellou_etal_1992 estimated _ FG = 5 12 rather than our estimate of _ FG = +12 2 .

Solid detections of faint secondary emission components were made in the brightest face-on galaxies of our sample, as shown in Figs. figure:n628smo - figure:n6946smo . In addition to the bright polarized emission that typically resides at a Faraday depth within only a few tens of rad m ^ 2 of the Galactic foreground value, much fainter polarized emission (by a factor of 4 -- 5) is detected in NGC 628, 5194, and 6946, which is offset to both positive and negative Faraday depths by about 200 rad m ^ 2 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 5194 as a nearby grand-design spiral with well-defined arms and finds no enhanced molecular-gas star-formation efficiency in its arm regions.

quotes.quote: We chose three galaxies, two grand-design (NGC 628 and NGC 5194) and one more flocculent (NGC 6946) on the basis of their proximity, orientation and multiband data. These have coverage in GALEX (NUV+FUV) (Gil de Paz et al., 2007), THINGS (Walter et al., 2008), SINGS (Kennicutt et al., 2003) and IRAM 30 observations (Schuster et al. 2007, Leroy et al., 2009).

We note that the grand-design spirals, NGC 5194 and NGC 628, have spiral arms that were well-defined using only the m=4 component divided by the m=0 component and that moving up to m=6 leads to little, if any difference.
Thus, at least for the two grand design spirals, NGC 628 and NGC 5194, we find that the SFE based on the molecular gas component is not enhanced in the arm region.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5194/M51 as the Whirlpool Galaxy with a weak AGN, describes inner molecular-tracer imaging, and reports its first H2CO detection.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 (M51): The ``Whirlpool Galaxy'' is a well-studied Sbc galaxy with a weak AGN. The inner 2. ^ 5 of this galaxy have been imaged in a variety of molecular tracers, including CO, ^ 13 CO, and C ^ 18 O J= 1-0 ( Kohno1996 ; Aalto1999 ), CO J= 2-1 Schinnerer2010 , and HCN and HCO ^+ J= 1-0 ( Kohno1996 ; Schinnerer2010 ). These dense gas studies have revealed structure associated with the nearly face-on spiral arms down to spatial scales 120 pc.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 5194's dust is mainly heated by star-forming regions, its molecular gas and attenuation trace FIR emission, and its optical-depth level matches the sample.

quotes.quote: The recent results of on NGC 5194 also suggest that the dust is mainly heated by star forming regions. Hence, in NGC 4254, NGC 4321, and NGC 5194, the molecular gas and most of the dust attenuation is associated with the FIR emission near regions of star formation.
NGC 5194 is close to the value for the entire sample.

We see that there is a tight correlation between the total gas surface density derived with a modified black body and from the combination of HI and CO observation as shown by the high value of the Spearman correlation coefficient for NGC 2403 ( =0.79 ), NGC 4254 ( =0.95 ), NGC 4321 ( =0.96 ), and NGC 5194 ( =0.95 ).

sources.summary: The paper fits NGC 5194 as heavily absorbed with an iron line but deems its Swift photon index and X-ray luminosity unreliable because it is Compton-thick.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 We find the spectrum of NGC 5194 a heavily absorbed power law with and a Gaussian to be the best fit to the Swift data. Previous spectral studies of NGC 5194 have also found it to be well fit by a heavily absorbed power law, with a strong Fe emission line Terashima2002, Ptak1999 . As the source is Compton thick, however, we cannot constrain the absorption well with the Swift data; we obtain an extremely low photon index ( = 0.7 ), significantly lower than that found in previous studies. We therefore conclude that both the value of and the x-ray luminosity we obtain are unreliable and consequently do not include the results of the spectral fits to NGC 5194 in subsequent analysis.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5194/M51a as a weakly interacting unbarred Sbc galaxy with LINER-like central excitation, a nuclear bar, and a relatively flat gradient.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 (M 51a) shows LINER like excitation in its centre (previously discussed in blanc2009 ).

Similarly, the gradient for the weakly interacting unbarred Sbc galaxy NGC 5194 is 0.09 0.01 dex ( R / ) ^ 1 , compared to -0.44 0.13 dex ( R / ) ^ 1 for the isolated unbarred galaxies (NGC 0628, 3938, 4254).

NGC 5194 (M51a) and NGC 1068 do not appear to host large-scale stellar bars, but do host nuclear bars. The small size of nuclear bars compared to the total size of the disc in a galaxy limits their ability to radially redistribute gas, so we classify these galaxies as ``Unbarred.''

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5194 as a local main-sequence comparison, finding its clump sizes fall below the F17 VDI scaling relation, consistent with a stable disk.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:all_points also shows the local main sequence galaxy NGC 5194 (whirlpool galaxy, M51a) in H and I band maps obtained from the Advanced Camera for Surveys on board the HST Mutchler2005AAS...206.1307M . Using the measurements of r_ disk , _ gas and V from Leroy2008AJ....136.2782L we notice that NGC 5194 lies below the F17 VDI scaling relation. This is in agreement with Leroy2008AJ....136.2782L who find a median value of Q 2-3 indicating a stable disk (except for in the dense regions of the spiral arms).
Two of upper limits seem to lie somewhat below the relation. This might be explained by the fact that these systems are, in fact, rather stable disks ( Q>1 ), similarly to NGC 5194.

sources.summary: The paper notes earlier profile fits were poor for NGC 5194/M 51, models its gas Toomre parameter declining to unity, and finds velocity dispersion below observations, perhaps from interaction.

quotes.quote: The fits of radial profiles were acceptable with reduced ^2 minimizations (defined as the normalized sum of the squared differences divided by the observational uncertainties) generally smaller than 2 for all galaxies except NGC 5194 (M 51): ^2_ tot = _ i ^2_ i = _ i O _i - M _i ^2 ^2_ i \ , where O _i corresponds to a single observational measurement, M _i is the model value, and _i the observational error.
The NGC 5194 model yields a Toomre parameter, which decreases from Q_ gas =2 in the center to about unity at R=1.5 kpc The radial profiles of the mass accretion rate generally increase with radius.

In NGC 5194, _ disp 8 km s ^ 1 , significantly lower than the observed velocity dispersion of 15 km s ^ 1 . The high observed velocity dispersion could be due to non-circular motions induced by the gravitational interaction with NGC 5195.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5194 as a massive spiral with elevated H I velocity dispersion and the highest quoted q value among the stellar-dominated galaxies.

quotes.quote: We derive the oblateness parameter q of the dark matter halo of a sample of gas rich, face-on disk galaxies. We have assumed that the halos are triaxial in shape but their axes in the disk plane (a and b) are equal, so that q=/a measures the halo flattening. We have used the velocity dispersion, derived from the stacked emission lines and the disk surface density to determine the disk potential and the halo shape at the R_ 25 and 1.5R_ 25 radii. We have applied our model to 20 nearby galaxies, of which 6 are large disk galaxies with M(stellar) > 10 ^ 10 , 8 have moderate stellar masses and 6 are low surface brightness dwarf galaxies. Our most important result is that gas rich galaxies that have M(gas)/M(baryons) > 0.5 have oblate halos ( q<0.55 ), whereas stellar dominated galaxies have a range of q values from 0.21 0.07 in NGC4190 to 1.27 0.61 in NGC5194.

We also included two galaxies NGC 4190 and UGC 8833 from the VLA ACS Nearby Galaxy Survey Treasury (VLA ANGST) survey ott.etal.2012 . Table 1 shows the sample of 20 galaxies, of which first 8 are relatively large, massive spirals (NGC 0628, NGC 3184, NGC 4736, NGC 5194, NGC 5236 and NGC 6946), and the remaining 12 are smaller spiral, dwarf or irregular galaxies. The procedure for determining the velocity dispersion across the galaxy disks as a function of radius is described in ianjamasimanana12 and ianjamasimanana.etal.2017 as well as in das.etal.2020 . We basically co-added the individual velocity profiles over radial bins to obtain azimuthally averaged, high signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) velocity dispersion values for each radial bin. The stacked profiles were fitted with single Gaussian functions, where the half width of the fitted Gaussian represents the velocity dispersion of the gas. This method gives a better estimate of the velocity dispersion compared to the moment2 maps. Figure 4 in the appendix shows the radial variation of the velocity dispersion across the galaxy disks. Its value is less than 10 kms ^ 1 at the R _ 25 radius, except for a few cases (NGC 5194, NGC 4190, DDO 187 and IC 10), where _ H i lies between 11 and 14 kms ^ 1 .

It is interesting to note that the gas rich galaxies (9 of them) all have oblate halos, with q<0.6 but the stellar dominated galaxies have a range of q values with q 0.2 (very oblate) to q 1.25 (prolate). Perhaps the most important result from this study is that the gas rich galaxies all have oblate halos at R_ 25 (Fig. 3). There are 9 gas rich galaxies that have M(gas)/M(baryon>0.5 and they are all dwarf galaxies : Holmberg1, Holmberg2, M81dwfA, DDO46, DDO53, DDO75, DDO187, F564-V3 and UGC8833. The q varies between 0.11 in DDO187 to 0.55 in Holmberg2. The stellar dominated galaxies, however, have a variety of q values ranging from q=0.21 in IC10 to q=1.25 for NGC5194 at R_ 25 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 5194 as a grand-design interacting spiral whose outer H I tail and satellite make the velocity field complex and anisotropic.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 This is a grand design spiral galaxy with a close satellite with which it interacts. The VRM analysis is limited to the galactic disk, R<300" , a region that extends beyond the optical radius R_ 25 230'' . The galaxy at larger radii is characterized by the presence of the satellite and a long tail of HI, making the velocity field complex and dominated by large anisotropic fluctuations due to strong tidal effects. The TRM finds that the inclination angle is in the range of 20^ < i <50^ , with a peculiar "bump" at small radii (see Fig.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5194 with M 51, describing its grand-design arms, central AGN-dominated radio continuum, nuclear molecular-gas gap, and arm-enhanced ratios.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 . The Whirlpool galaxy M 51 is the prototypical grand design spiral galaxy, with two prominent spiral arms where the molecular gas is generally confined. The arms wind in towards the center of the galaxy but remain in two distinct peaks; the molecular gas lacks emission right at the nucleus. The RC at the nucleus is dominated by the AGN activity. The \ image is remarkable in that it shows strong enhancements along the spiral arms, as discussed in 4.3.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5194 as M 51, a face-on Seyfert 2 spiral whose interaction with NGC 5195 likely drove past intense star formation.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 (M 51) is a nearby grand-design spiral galaxy with a Seyfert 2 nucleus. The proximity of this galaxy as well as its nearly face-on orientation has made it the target of numerous large scale multi-wavelength studies Scoville83,Scoville01,Schuster07 . Its interaction with the companion galaxy NGC 5195 seems to have been the origin of a past period of intense star formation Thronson91,Greenawalt98 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC5194 as a Seyfert outlier with small 7.7/11.3 aromatic ratio, strong H2 emission, high 11.3/7.7 ratio beyond neutral-molecule models, and low AGN contribution.

quotes.quote: Inspection of Figure fig:spec reveals that several Seyferts with small L(7.7 m)/L(11.3 m) aromatic feature ratios also exhibit a strong H _2 S(3) rotational line at 9.67 m (e.g., NGC4501, NGC5194.)

We find that a number of Seyferts lie beyond the range of model predictions, even for completely neutral aromatic molecules; these are the 6/35 Seyferts with L(11.3 m)/L(7.7 m )>0.6 : NGC5194, NGC4501, NGC4639, NGC1433, NGC2639, and NGC5005.
The three obvious outliers, NGC2639, NGC4501, and NGC5194, all have / <0.25 , implying that the AGN contribution to \ is <10 e.g., stu02 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes X-ray spectra of NGC 5194, describing its best-fit components, Fe K emission or flux behavior, and comparison with previous modeling.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 The nuclear region of NGC 5194 contains several X-ray emitting features: the AGN and diffuse emission to the North and South (see Terashima et al. 2001). Chandra is necessary to isolate the Seyfert nucleus and we therefore present the results of the Chandra analysis only, and do not include the archival data from XMM-Newton .

sources.summary: The review treats NGC 5194 as a strong two-armed spiral whose CO-to-24 micron phase shifts agree with a density-wave interpretation over most of its spiral extent.

quotes.quote: It was found that two of the objects (NGC 628 and NGC 5194), with strong two-arm patterns, present positive evidence for long-lived spirals.
The results are shown in figure  fig1 , where we can see that only three objects (NGC 628, NGC 3627 and NGC 5194) show signs of strong m=2 spiral patterns.
For NGC 5194, the phase shifts agree with a DW scenario for most of its spiral extension (see figure  fig2 ).

sources.summary: The atlas gives M51a's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: M51a is a face-on grand-design spiral galaxy at a distance of D= 8.09 0.87 Mpc (NED redshift-independent median) interacting with the smaller M51b galaxy. It hosts a Sy 2 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 sometimes also classified as a LINER heckman_optical_1980 and possesses a biconical NLR, a weak radio jet ford_bubbles_1985,ho_radio_2001 and a nuclear water maser hagiwara_water_2001,hagiwara_low-luminosity_2007 . The first MIR observations of M51a were performed by and , whereas the nucleus remained undetected.

sources.summary: NGC 5194 is treated as the sample galaxy with the strongest interaction, matching encounter simulations by showing complex spiral structure and no clear gas-stellar azimuthal offset.

quotes.quote: The largest crosses denote the optical data for the three galaxies (NGC 3031,NGC 4321 and NGC 5194) which are apparently undergoing the strongest tidal interactions (i.e. with P > 10^ 3 (equation 1); see Table 4 and Figure 17).
In the case of spiral structure induced by a strong galactic encounter (as in the modeling of M51 - NGC 5194 - by Dobbs et al. 2010) no azimuthal offset was found between the gas and stellar spirals.

We find no systematic differences between the galactic properties in these three groups apart from the fact that all the galaxies in the F(locculent) category are isolated ( P < 10^ 5 ; see Table 2); the R(egular) and C(omplex) galaxies occupy a wide range of P values, although notably the galaxy which is undergoing by far the strongest interaction (NGC 5194) is in the C(omplex) category, in line with the simulations of this encounter by Dobbs et al. (2010).

sources.summary: The paper notes NGC 5194 (M51) is a merger, finds it close to a closed-box chemical-evolution case, and places it among low-accretion galaxies with a steep gas profile.

quotes.quote: We note that while the majority of our sample consists of isolated field galaxies, NGC 5194 (M51) is a merger and NGC 4625 is a pair with NGC 4618 (the latter is not included in our sample).

The galaxies NGC 5055 and 5194 shown in Fig. figure10 are very close to the closed-box case with very small and values (we note that the model distribution for NGC 5194 shows a too strong decline at larger radii similar to the cases discussed before).

For the low cases NGC 5194, 5055, 4321, 6946, 5457 we find _g = 1.4, 1.2 1.3, 1.2, 1.1  dex  R_ 25 ^ 1 , respectively (NGC 3938, another low accretion case but with a very high = 1.04 , is an exception with _g = 0.7  dex  R_ 25 ^ 1 ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5194 as M51, an interacting face-on spiral with H2O maser activity, old inner stellar populations, massive-star groups, and strong [S III] lines.

quotes.quote: NGC 5194 (S2) NGC 5194, or M51, is a face-on spiral galaxy that is interacting with a neighboring system, NGC 5195 e.g. BSmith10 . H _2 O maser activity is detected in this galaxy, although it is unclear whether it is associated with an obscuring torus, jet, or star-forming region Ho87,Hagiwara01,Hagiwara07 .

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 5194 among populous cluster catalogs used for mass-cut and age-limit tests, finding a shallow 10-100 Myr relation.

quotes.quote: We repeat the analysis in Section sec:delt after applying different mass cuts for the galaxies with enough statistics to do a proper study: NGC 1313, NGC 5194, NGC 628, and NGC 1566 to see if we see a trend for an increase in the age accompanying increases in the mass cut. Figure fig:masscut shows negligible increase in the maximum age of the t - R relation, about 10 or less, which is well within the errors.

Evolutionary effects on the clusters already appear to have impacted the age--separation results by 300 Myr, flattening the slopes and increasing the characteristic star formation timescale compared to clusters that are only 100 Myr old in NGC 628, NGC 5194, and NGC 1313.
The clusters in the range 10--100 Myr for both NGC 1313 and NGC 5194 show an extremely shallow relation compared to what is observed for the rest of the systems.

sources.summary: The paper describes M51A as the Whirlpool Galaxy, a nearby spiral with an elliptical companion, and finds that its SED does not require an AGN component.

quotes.quote: Group II: The Reference Sample M51A : M51A, also known as the Whirlpool Galaxy, is a well-known spiral galaxy with an elliptical companion. CIGALE modeled M51A with an AGN at 9 of the IR luminosity. her16 modeled it as an obscured AGN, while L13 calculated a best-fit model that did not have an AGN. Nothing in the SED demands the presence of an AGN.

sources.summary: The paper uses PAWS data for NGC 5194/M51 and finds CO-only sightlines and streaming-motion features along its spiral arms.

quotes.quote: For NGC 5194 (M51), we use the 1 resolution integrated CO(1-0) image from PAWS PdBI Arcsecond Whirlpool Survey, schinnerer13 .
Of the detected sightlines, CO only sightlines (dark blue) are the most common detections in more than half of our targets (namely NGC 0628, NGC 4254, NGC 4321, NGC 4535, NGC 5194).
A similar feature appears in NGC 5194, with CO only sightlines appearing along segments of the inner spiral arms features associated with strong streaming motions by meidt13 .

sources.summary: NGC5194 is analyzed with high-resolution CO(1-0) data; the paper compares its GMC lifetimes with M51-based estimates and notes agreement despite a recent interaction with NGC5195.

quotes.quote: We therefore use the CO(2-1) transition as a tracer of the molecular gas for all galaxies except NGC5194, for which only a CO(1-0) map is available at high resolution, observed by the Plateau de Bure Interferometer PdBI; Pety2013,Schinnerer2013 .
We note in particular that this is the case for NGC5194 Eufrasio2017,Tress2019 , despite its relatively recent interaction with NGC5195, 350-500 Myr ago Salo2000, MentuchCooper2012,Eufrasio2017 .
In Figure fig:Meidt15 , we compare the GMC lifetimes measured in this study with the cloud lifetimes inferred by Meidt2015 in NGC5194 (M51).

sources.summary: The paper uses PAWS CO(1-0) data for NGC5194, finds wind-time caveats unlikely to change conclusions, and notes supernovae could contribute to cloud dispersal.

quotes.quote: All galaxies presented here have been observed in CO(2-1) with the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array ALMA; see Leroy2020 as part of the PHANGS Physics at High Angular Resolution in Nearby GalaxieS; http://phangs.org . ALMA survey, except for NGC5194, observed in CO(1-0) by the PAWS survey Pety2013, Schinnerer2013 .

This might have a significant impact only for NGC5194, and even then it is unlikely to change our conclusions, given the large difference between t_ phot and t_ wind (3.2 Myr and 8.7 Myr, respectively, see Table tab:timescales ).
For NGC4254 and NGC5194, the supernova delay time is smaller than the observed feedback time-scale, such that supernova explosions could contribute to cloud dispersal.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5194 (M 51) as a specific thermal-correction case when comparing magnetic-field estimates in nearby galaxy radio-continuum maps.

quotes.quote: In Fig.  fig:thermal_diff the difference between the magnetic field strengths B = B_ eq B_ nt is shown for the specific case of NGC 5194 (M 51). The difference in the estimate of the magnetic field is below the 1 difference and B_ eq is smaller than B_ nt for most parts of the galaxy. Thus, the non-thermally corrected estimate on the magnetic field strength gives a lower bound of the real strength, but lies within the uncertainties.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5194 as a transition object with ongoing star formation, arguing that observed near-infrared [Fe II] emission is consistent with shock-heated dust.

quotes.quote: In order to fit the galaxies that exhibit ongoing star formation, such as a transition object NGC 5194 (Heckman 1980; Larkin et al. 1998), we tried to add a shock wave (that may be related with the supernova explosions and/or outflowing winds from starburst) as additional mechanism for heating the dust, following Dwek (1986) and Contini et al. (1998).

It is worth noting that a strong near-infrared [ Fe ii ] line has been observed in NGC 5194, and the shock excitation in supernova remnants is probably the mechanism responsible for this line (Larkin et al 1998). This excitation mechanism may be consistent with our consideration of shock-heating of dust in this galaxy, with v_ s in order 100 km s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5194 as a Seyfert with enhanced nuclear HCN emission, a roughly 100 pc dense obscuring envelope, very high HCN/CO ratio, and kpc-scale jet or outflow activity.

quotes.quote: A striking enhancement of HCN(1 0) emission has been reported in the nuclear regions of some Seyfert galaxies such as NGC 1068 and NGC 5194 (Kohno et al. 1996 and references therein), implying extreme physical conditions of the molecular gas near active nuclei ( e.g. Matsushita et al. 1998).

We suggest that the observed R_ HCN/CO in Seyfert galaxies may be related to the size of dense obscuring material ; NGC 1068 and NGC 5194 have a relatively extended ( 100 pc scale) envelope of obscuring material, which consists of very dense interstellar medium and therefore shows extremely high R_ HCN/CO .
Note that the ``high R_ HCN/CO Seyferts'', i.e. NGC 1068, NGC 3079, and NGC 5194, possess kpc scale jet/outflows.

gmw_00000127.name: M81
gmw_00000127.overview: M81 is a nearby grand-design spiral galaxy [arXiv:1710.04050, arXiv:2305.05650] that hosts a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2106.04041, arXiv:2312.01328] classified as a LINER [arXiv:astro-ph/0702292, arXiv:1302.5011]. The galaxy is the central member of an interacting group [arXiv:1205.3559, arXiv:2109.02663], showing tidal features and altered star-formation activity from encounters with its companions [arXiv:1002.0456, arXiv:1803.01856].

sources.summary: The paper maps M81 outskirts, finding young stellar systems and tidal debris tied to its disk, H I arms, and interactions with nearby group galaxies.

quotes.quote: Using the MegaCam imager on the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope, we have resolved individual stars in the outskirts of the nearby large spiral galaxy M81 (NGC 3031) well below the tip of the red giant branch of metal-poor stellar populations over 60 58 . In this paper, we report the discovery of new young stellar systems in the outskirts of M81. The most prominent feature is a chain of clumps of young stars distributed along the extended southern H i tidal arm connecting M 81 and NGC 3077.

Beyond the young disk of M81, a number of stellar concentrations distributed over a much larger scale are visible, of which a few have been already reported in the literature, i.e., Holmberg IX, BK 3N, Garland, M81-West, Arp's Loop (A0952+69), and the tidal debris objects recently reported by davidge08 . In addition to the structure combining M81-West and the first object in the list of davidge08 , tracing the continuation of the H i spiral structure of M81, a second arm of young stars (M81-West2) tracing the continuation of the second H i spiral structure is visible. Similar structures of young stars tracing the continuation of the H i spiral structures (M81-SE) are observed to the south along the major axis. Wide field GALEX images of M81 gildepaz07 show that these structures of young star candidates are sites of Ultra-Violet emission. More strikingly, a number of previously unknown overdensities of young star candidates can be seen along the tidal bridge of gas stretching from M81 to its neighbour NGC 3077. Interestingly, BK3N and Garland appear to lie at the two extremities of this chain of concentrations of young stars, close to M81 and NGC 3077 respectively.

sources.summary: The paper uses AKARI far-infrared maps of M 81 to trace dust temperature, optical depth, radial gradients, and warm star-forming regions along the spiral arms.

quotes.quote: The importance of the far-infrared (FIR) mapping is demonstrated for a face-on spiral galaxy, M 81, by analyzing its imaging data at 65, 90, and 140 taken by AKARI . Basic products are the dust temperature map, the dust optical depth map, and the colour--colour diagram. The main features are as follows. (i) The dust temperature derived from the total fluxes at 90 and 140 reflects the relatively low temperatures seen in the interarm and spiral arms excluding the warm spots, rather than the high temperatures in warm spots and the centre. This indicates that the total FIR luminosity is dominated by the dust heated by the general interstellar radiation field.

(ii) The galaxy is more extended at 140 than at the other shorter wavelengths, which reflects the radial dust temperature gradient. (iii) The dust optical depth derived from the FIR mapping is broadly consistent with that estimated from the FIR-to-ultraviolet luminosity ratio. (iv) The FIR colour--colour diagram is useful to identify a `contamination' of warm dust. The existence of small-scale warm star-forming regions is supported in the bright spots along the spiral arms.

sources.summary: The paper describes M81 as the central, most massive M81 Group galaxy and uses its halo, distance, and relation to newly found remote globular clusters.

quotes.quote: The main galaxy located in the center of the group is M81, surrounded by 29 member galaxies chi09,mak11 . According to mak11 , the systemic velocity with respect to the Local Group and the velocity dispersion of the M81 Group are v_ LG = 193 km s ^ 1 and _v=138 km s ^ 1 , respectively, and the total mass derived from the velocity of the members is M= 3.89 10^ 12 M_ . The M81 Group includes also a famous starburst galaxy M82. kar06 estimate from kinematics and distances of the member galaxies that M81 is twice as massive as M82 and the mass of the M81 Group is 77 of the mass of the Local Group. The distance to M81 is known to be 3.63 0.14 Mpc ( (m-M)_0= 27.80 0.08 ) derived using the tip of the red giant branch (TRGB) method from deep HST images as well as Cepheids by dur10 .

GC-2 lies 406 97 kpc behind M81 along our line of sight, according to our TRGB distance estimates to GC-2 and Field-2. Considering this and the projected distance in the sky we derive a three-dimensional distance from M81, 408 97 kpc. Although it is closer to M82 than M81 in the sky, its three dimension distance is significantly larger than the projected distance from M82 (also from M81). Also M81 is twice as massive as M82 and is the most massive member in the M81 Group.

sources.summary: The paper studies M81's stellar halo with HST GHOSTS fields, tracing populations to 50 kpc and finding old, moderately metal-poor halo stars with no detectable color gradient beyond 15 kpc.

quotes.quote: We study the properties of the stellar populations in M81's outermost part, which hereafter we will term the stellar halo, using Hubble Space Telescope (HST) Advanced Camera for Surveys observations of 19 fields from the GHOSTS survey. The observed fields probe the stellar halo out to a projected distance of 50 kpc from the galactic center. Each field was observed in both F606W and F814W filters. The 50 completeness levels of the color magnitude diagrams (CMDs) are typically at 2 mag below the tip of the red giant branch (TRGB). Fields at distances closer than 15 kpc show evidence of disk-dominated populations whereas fields at larger distances are mostly populated by halo stars.

The RGB of the M81's halo CMDs is well matched with isochrones of 10 Gyr and metallicities [ Fe/H ] 1.2 dex, suggesting that the dominant stellar population of M81's halo has a similar age and metallicity. The halo of M81 is characterized by a color distribution of width 0.4 mag and an approximately constant median value of ( F606W - F814W ) 1 mag measured using stars within the magnitude range 23.7 F814W 25.5 . When considering only fields located at galactocentric radius R > 15 kpc, we detect no color gradient in the stellar halo of M81.

sources.summary: NGC 3031/M81 is treated as a bright nearby LLAGN whose broad H line, jet inclination, SED models, and optical stellar population constrain its accretion geometry.

quotes.quote: NGC 3031 This source is among the brightest LLAGNs known since it is the nearest AGN besides Centaurus A and has been the subject of a broadband multiwavelength monitoring campaign Markoff08, Miller10 . Devereux07 modeled the profile of the broad double-peaked H line with a relativistic thin disk model. They were able to explain the profile with an inclination angle of 50^ . Such a high inclination angle is supported by radio observations of the jet Bietenholz00 . The H line profile of M81 is consistent with an inner radius of 280 - 360 R_S for the line-emitting thin disk Devereux07 .

In our models for the SED, we therefore adopt i=50^ and r_ tr = 360 . The resulting IR-luminous thin disk spectrum is not strongly constrained by the available IR upper limits and optical-UV observations. On the other hand, the optical data points at 6000 \ and 4700 \ are consistent with a stellar population with mass 6.2 10^7 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper presents M81 as a nearby interacting spiral whose SNR spectra show bimodal line ratios, spiral-arm locations, disk kinematics, and radial abundance gradients.

quotes.quote: M81 (NGC 3031) is another nearby bright spiral (SA(s)ab) galaxy, located beyond the Local Group. It is a main member of the M81 Group, interacting with the well-known starburst galaxy M82 and a dwarf galaxy NGC 3077 with morphology type of I0. mat97 provided a list of 41 SNR candidates selected using the criterion of / 0.45 from the and images of M81.

The distribution of / ratios of M81 SNRs is bimodal. M81 SNRs are divided into two groups in the spectral line ratio diagrams: an strong group and an weak group. The latter have larger sizes, and may have faster shock velocity. / ratios of the SNRs show a strong correlation with / ratios. They show a clear radial gradient in / and / ratios: dLog ( / )/dLog R = -0.018 0.008 dex kpc ^ 1 and dLog ( / )/dLog R =-0.016 0.008 dex kpc ^ 1 where R is a deprojected galactocentric distance.

sources.summary: The GHOSTS analysis treats NGC 3031 as one of the nearest sample galaxies, measuring median RGB halo colours per HST field and finding a rather flat minor-axis stellar-halo colour profile.

quotes.quote: For the three nearest galaxies (NGC 0253, NGC 3031, and NGC 4945) we obtain a median colour measurement per HST field. These galaxies are at a distance of 4 Mpc and the size of their HST fields extends over roughly the same linear distance, covering 3.5 and 2.8 kpc on a side of the ACS and WFC3 field of view (FoV), respectively.

We assume that the stars observed along the minor axis fields located at galactocentric distances R> 5 kpc for the edge-on galaxies (NGC 0253, NGC 0891, NGC 4565, NGC 4945) and R > 10 kpc for the highly inclined galaxies (NGC 3031, NGC 7814) represent halo stellar populations. Figure fig:colprof_minmaj shows the minor axis stellar halo colour profile of each galaxy. In order to give a rough quantitative estimate of the magnitude of colour variation with radius, we fit a linear colour gradient to the data. Such a function has no particular physical relevance or motivation, and a variety of radial profile shapes are predicted by models e.g. Cooper10, Font11, Tissera14 . Other parameterizations are possible, but additional complexity seems unwarranted given the number of data points and their uncertainties.

Half of the galaxies (NGC 4565, NGC 0891, and NGC 7814) show fits consistent with stellar halo colour gradients whereas the remaining three galaxies (NGC 0253, NGC 3031, and NGC 4945) have rather flat colour profile.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes M81 in detail, finding a Galactic-absorbed power-law X-ray spectrum and spectral variability most likely driven by photon-index changes.

quotes.quote: M81 (NGC 3031) As the most extensively observed object to show harder-when-brighter behaviour, the spectra of M81 were studied in the greatest detail. The soft component of M81 in particular, extrapolated from its colour-colour plots (see section colour ), was derived in order to be able to draw conclusions about similar sources in the sample. Fig. m81spectra shows a sample of the spectra from M81, covering the full observed flux range. The X-ray spectrum of M81 is well described by a simple power law absorbed by the Galactic absorbing column of 7.2 10^ 21 cm^ 2 . This is consistent with the many previous shorter-term X-ray studies of M81 e.g. Elvis1982,Swartz2003,Markoff2008 ; past studies discovered narrow features attributed to high-ionisation emission lines including Fe lines e.g. Ishisaki1996,Pellegrini2000,Page2003,Page2004 , but these are not detectable in the Swift data.

The data therefore require a constant hot gas component, but it does not contribute to the spectrum enough to explain the overall spectral variability. It is therefore most likely that the observed spectral variability is driven by changes to . The top-left panel in Fig. allGamma shows how changes with increasing flux for the best-fitting model to M81 (model 6).

sources.summary: The LOFAR study targeted M81, described its distance and suitability for LOFAR, and found no pulsars or transients above new low-frequency limits.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we describe the searches we have performed with LOFAR for pulsars in spiral Triangulum Galaxy M33 (distance d 0.73-0.94 Mpc ), Bode's Galaxy M81 ( d 3.50-3.74 Mpc ), and starburst Cigar Galaxy M82 ( d 3.5-3.8 Mpc ). Given their northern positions (see Table Obs ), these three galaxies can be well targeted with LOFAR, as the projected effective area of LOFAR's fixed dipoles, and thus the sensitivity, is the highest close to zenith see Leeuwen-2010 . The origin and current environment of a galaxy inevitably affect its star-formation rate and mass-energy distribution. As a result, the pulsar population in nearby galaxies can be different from that of our Milky Way, and a comparison between this population and that of our Milky Way is interesting.

We coherently combined at most 23 LOFAR Core High-Band Antenna (HBA) stations and covered M33, M81, and M82 in their entirety using multiple tied-array beams. No pulsating sources or single pulses were found. We have, therefore established stricter limits on the extragalactic pulsar flux density at lower frequencies than those obtained in previous Arecibo , GBT, and WSRT searches. We conclude that in nearby galaxies M33, M81, and M82 there are no pulsars shining toward Earth with pseudo luminosities greater than a few times that of the brightest pulsars in our Milky Way.

sources.summary: The paper places M81 in Group II, notes it has no bar, requires a large turbulence correlation time, and finds observed pitch angles much larger than the model.

quotes.quote: Group II consists of M81, NGC 253, NGC 1566, NGC 6946, and IC 342. M81 has no bar whereas the others are SAB galaxies but with a small bar. In each case, the bar does not extend to the midpoint of the inner radial range considered.
Only for one galaxy, M81, the required value exceeds 30 , so we set to 30 for it. The resulting magnetic pitch angles in the saturated state of the dynamo are shown in Column 8 of Table tab:B_data .

M81 The observed |p| of M81 are much larger than those found from the model (though because of large formal uncertainties in the M81 pitch angle data, does not improve significantly if M81 is excluded). M81 is the only galaxy where the large-scale magnetic field may be predominantly non-axisymmetric Krause+89,Sokoloff+92,Fletcher10 , in which case our model would not be expected to produce good agreement for this galaxy. However, the observations are rather old, and their interpretation relied on incomplete procedures, so that the question of the global magnetic field structure in this galaxy remains open.

sources.summary: The paper presents multiwavelength observations of the low-luminosity AGN M81*, measuring a 2011 radio flare, delayed X-ray/radio behavior, magnetic-field limits, and a mildly relativistic VLBA knot.

quotes.quote: M81* is one of the nearest low-luminosity jets, which underwent an extremely large radio flare in 2011, allowing us to study compact core emission with unprecedented sensitivity and linear resolution. Utilizing a multi-wavelength campaign, we were able to track the flare as it re-brightened and became optically thick. Simultaneous X-ray observations indicated the radio re-brightening was preceded by a low energy X-ray flare at least t_ delay >12 \ days prior.

In addition, VLBA observations at 23 GHz clearly illustrate a discrete knot moving mildly relativistically at v_ app /=0.51 0.17 associated with the initial radio flare. The observations indicate radial jet motions for the first time in M81*. This has profound implications for jet production, as it means radial motion can be observed in even the lowest-luminosity AGN, but at slower velocities and smaller radial extents ( 10^4 \ R_ G ).

M81*, at a distance of 3.96 0.29 Mpc , is one of the nearest low-luminosity active galactic nuclei (AGN), and has a black hole mass of 7^ +2 _ 1 10^7 \ M_ . It has been well surveyed at many frequencies, including radio and X-ray .

sources.summary: The paper treats M81/NGC 3031 as the closest LLAGN host; Suzaku/NuSTAR spectra constrain M81* reflection, Fe emission, black-hole mass, and sub-Eddington accretion.

quotes.quote: We present the results of simultaneous Suzaku and NuSTAR observations of the nearest Low-Luminosity Active Galactic Nucleus (LLAGN), M81 ^ * . The spectrum is well described by a cut-off power law plus narrow emission lines from Fe K , Fe xxv and Fe xxvi . There is no evidence of Compton reflection from an optically thick disc, and we obtain the strongest constraint on the reflection fraction in M81 ^* to date, with a best-fit value of R = 0.0 with an upper limit of R < 0.1 .

M81, also known as NGC 3031, is a spiral galaxy hosting the closest Low-Luminosity Active Galactic Nucleus (LLAGN), M81 ^ * , at a distance of 3.63 Mpc Freedman1994 . Its central black hole has a mass of M = 7 10^7 M_ Devereux2003 . The bolometric luminosity of M81 ^* is 9.3 10^ 40 erg s ^ 1 Ho1996 , which corresponds to 1.1 10^ 5 L_ Edd , where L_ Edd is the Eddington luminosity. Thus, logarithmically speaking, M81 ^ * is midway between ultra-low Eddington fraction accreters such as Sgr A* at the centre of the Galaxy with L_ Sgr \ A* 3 10^ 10 L_ Edd Melia2001 and a typical high-efficiency AGN with L_ AGN 0.1 - 1 L_ Edd . M81 ^* is particularly interesting to study because it is nearby and bright enough to allow detailed spectroscopy, while being in the sub-Eddington regime in which the accretion flow is thought to be qualitatively different to that of an efficiently radiating, high accretion rate AGN.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC3031 as a worked example for bulge-disk fitting, describing its smooth surface-brightness profile, sky residuals, and constrained dust opacity.

quotes.quote: In Figs.  fig:NGC3031 and fig:NGC4826 we show examples of the fitting steps for galaxies NGC3031 and NGC4826 to illustrate the whole procedure described here. For NGC3031, the observed average surface brightness profile was smooth up to R_ max , without deviations from an exponential profile. Therefore, for this galaxy (as was the case for a few others in our sample), Step 3 fit was not necessary and the corresponding plots are not shown.

We have used the positive sky residuals in the outer parts of galaxies (such as the one noticeable in the surface brightness profiles of NGC4826 or even NGC3031 towards R_ max and beyond) to estimate the systematic errors in bulge-to-disk ratios.

For three galaxies in our sample, namely NGC3031, NGC4594 and NGC4736, the initial value derived for _ B ^ f is higher than 8.0 the upper limit of the numerical corrections for dust effects given in and . These values are unrealistically large. This could be due to either an overestimate of the stellar mass, or issues with the fit/sky determination (especially for the case of NGC4594) resulting in a larger value for R_ s,d . Therefore, for these 3 galaxies we have chosen to fix the value of the dust opacity to 3.80 (see Table tab:dust ), the average value found by .

sources.summary: The paper builds a 3D radiative-transfer model of M 81, describing it as an interacting early-type spiral where old stellar populations dominate central dust heating.

quotes.quote: In this work, we will describe this modelling approach and apply it to the early-type spiral galaxy M 81 (NGC 3031, Sab, Hubble stage = 2.4, D 3.7 Mpc, i 59 deg). M 81 is an interacting galaxy e.g., Casasola2004 , belonging to the M 81 group consisting of 34 members. The gas content of M 81 has been extensively studied e.g., Combes1977, Sakamoto2001, Casasola2007, Heiner2008, SanchezGallego2011 because of the strong density wave attributed to the tidal interaction with the companions of the M 81 group, especially M 82 and NGC 3077 Kaufman1989 .

We obtain a realistic 3D radiative transfer model of the face-on galaxy M 81. We find that only 50.2 of the dust heating can be attributed to young stellar populations ( 100 Myr ). We confirm a tight correlation between the specific star formation rate and the heating fraction by young stellar populations, both in sky projection and in 3D, also found for radiative transfer models of M 31 and M 51. We conclude that the old stellar populations can be a major contributor to the heating of dust. In M 81, old stellar populations are the dominant heating agent in the central regions and they contribute to half of the absorbed radiation.

sources.summary: The paper studies M81's circumnuclear ionized gas, identifying its LLAGN, nuclear spiral, ionized gas mass, possible biconical outflow, and LINER/Seyfert nuclear classifications.

quotes.quote: Here we present a detailed study of ionized gas in the central kiloparsec region of M81, which hosts the closest prototype low-luminosity active galactic nucleus, based on optical integral-field spectroscopic observations taken with the CAHA 3.5m telescope. It is found that much of the circumnuclear ionized gas is concentraed within a bright core of 200 pc in extent and a surrounding spiral-like structure known as the nuclear spiral. The total mass of the ionized gas is estimated to be 2 10^5 M_ , which corresponds to a few percent of the cold gas mass in this region, as traced by co-spatial dust extinction features. Plausible signature of a bi-conical outflow along the disk plane is suggested by a pair of blueshifted/redshifted low-velocity features, symmetrically located at 120 -- 250 pc from the nucleus. The spatially-resolved line ratios of [ N ii ] /H and [ O iii ] /H demonstrate that much of the circumnuclear region can be classified as LINER (low-ionization nuclear emission-line region).

At a distance of 3.6 Mpc (1 corresponds to 17.5 pc; 2001ApJ...553...47F ), M81 hosts the nearest prototype LLAGN, known as M81* 1999ApJ...516..672H , which has a bolometric luminosity of 2 10 ^ 41 erg s ^ 1 , corresponding to an Eddington ratio of 3 10 ^ 5 2014MNRAS.438.2804N for an estimated SMBH mass of 7 10^7 M _ 2003AJ....125.1226D . Thanks to its proximity, the central region of M81 has been extensively investigated in multi-wavelengths and classified as both LINER 1980A&A....87..152H and type-1 Seyfert 1981ApJ...245..845P , the property of which was reviewed in . AGN activities are evidenced by a variable, power-law X-ray continuum 1996PASJ...48..237I , a featureless, compact UV continuum 1996ApJ...462..183H, 1997ApJ...481L..71D , optical double-peaked broad emission lines 1996AJ....111.1901B , and a compact radio core plus a one-sided, precessing jet 2001MNRAS.321..767I, 2011A&A...533A.111M .

sources.summary: The paper describes M 81 as the dominant spiral in its group and reports J-PLUS globular-cluster candidates forming a possible bridge from M 81 toward M 82.

quotes.quote: M 81 is a spiral located at 3.6 Mpc tully2013 , being the dominant galaxy of a group conformed by 30 members. The foreground extinction in the direction of M 81 is 0.16 in the -band ( Schlafly_Finkbeiner2011 ). It is classified as a spiral with a classical bulge by fisher2008 , and its bulge mass is huge given its stellar halo mass, occupying an unusual region in the bulge mass-stellar halo mass diagram in the sample of bell2017 . It presents a stellar mass of 3-8.5 10^ 10 M_ ( kk2014 and oehm2017 ) and a dark matter halo mass of 1 10^ 12 M_ , ( oehm2017 ).

While most of the previously known GCs are close to M 81, the new GC candidates appear to be distributed across the entire region. The count distribution of GC candidates with over-plotted density contours shown in the right panel of fig:spatial reveals an overdensity of GC candidates extending from M 81 in the direction of M 82. Such count excess provides tantalising evidence of a GC bridge. Although a bridge of H i gas between M 81 and M 82 is evident from , see fig:triplet , a bridge of GCs between these galaxies has not yet been reported.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 3031 as a TRGB training case, comparing edge-detection responses across its fields and identifying which halo positions produce the best tip measurements.

quotes.quote: A Case Study of TRGB Detection with NGC 3031 We focus on NGC 3031 as a ``training set'' to establish a robust algorithm and then we will apply it to other galaxies. In Fig. fig:EDR3031 , we show the Edge-Detection Response for a nominal analysis case with Hatt style weighting and both spatial and color clipping applied as previously described. We show the responses for two different smoothing values ( =0.05, 0.10 ).

When considering which fields will yield the best TRGB measurements, it is interesting to consider the example of the spatial positions of the fields in NGC 3031 and Figure fig:fields . Top-quality measures ( R 7 and 200 ) are available only in three of the eleven Halo fields 01, 04, and 08. Fields 02 and 10 have only fair contrast ( R 4 - 6 with field 02 having two possible tips separated by 0.2 mag) and 05 and 11 have fair contrast and low ( < 200 ). The rest, fields 03, 06, 07, 09 are of low-quality and do not yield a reliable tip measurement. We find the best detection come from the region just outside the disk (in this case just beyond the 25th mag/sq. arcsec isophot and at a projected radius if 15-20 kpc) with further fields poorly constrained.

sources.summary: The paper presents M81 as a nearby spiral with a low-luminosity AGN, compact core and jet, and evidence for a 30-year supermassive black-hole binary in M81*.

quotes.quote: We report evidence that a supermassive black-hole binary with an orbital period of 30 years may be resident in the nearby galactic nucleus M81. This orbital period and the known mass of M81 imply an orbital separation of 0.02 parsecs. The jet emanating from the primary black hole showed a short period of jet wobbling 16.7 years, superposing a long-term precession at a time-scale of several hundred years. Periodic radio and X-ray outbursts were also found twice per orbital period, which could be explained by a double-peaked mass-accretion-rate variation per binary orbit. If confirmed, M81 would be one of the closest supermassive black-hole binary candidate, providing a rare opportunity to study the ``final parsec problem".

The nearby 3.96 Mpc 2007ApJ...668...924B spiral galaxy M81 (NGC 3031), harbors one of the nearest low luminosity active galactic nuclei 2002A&A...392..53N, 2008ARA&A...46...475H . The almost face-on aspect with an inclination angle to the line of sight (LOS) 14 2 degrees of the host galaxy allows a clear and unobscured view of the nucleus. With a black hole mass M 7 10^7M_ determined spectroscopically 2003AJ...125...1226D with the Hubble Space Telescope (HST), an angular resolution of 1 micro-arcsecond ( as) corresponds to a linear size of 6 gravitational radius ( r_g= GM ^2 ).

sources.summary: The paper uses M81 to show that disk and bulge X-ray binary luminosity functions differ, interpreting the disk break absence as continuous, smooth star formation.

quotes.quote: However, when the X-ray sources in the disk and in the bulge of M81 are considered separately, very different log N( > S) -- log S curves are obtained (Fig. 4, Tennant et al. \ 2001). The luminosity break is obvious in the log N( > S) -- log S curve of the bulge sources, but the log N( > S) -- log S curve of the disk sources is a single power law. Interestingly, the power-law slope of the disk sources is similar to that of the bulge sources below the luminosity break.

The absence of the break in the log N( > S) -- log S curve of the disk sources in M81 is inconsistent with the proposition that the luminosity break is universal for all galaxies. The locations of the breaks at different luminosities for early- and late-type galaxies (cf. \ M81 and NGC 4697, see Tennant et al. \ 2001 and Sarazin, Irwin Bregman 2000 respectively) suggest that the natures of the breaks are different for galaxies with different Hubble types. We propose that for some galaxies the luminosity break is determined by the formation and evolutionary history of the populations of X-ray binaries in the galaxy. The different morphologies for the log N( > S) -- log S curves for the disk and bulge sources in the spiral galaxies reflect the different star-formation histories for the galactic components and the different evolutionary paths of their X-ray binary populations.
By assuming a continual formation process, the model also explains the absence of a luminosity break for the disk sources in M81.

sources.summary: The paper uses SN 1993J light echoes to infer M81 disk dust sheets, HI-associated material, disk geometry, and fragmented interstellar structures above the plane.

quotes.quote: Using the technique of PSF-matched image subtraction, we have analyzed archival HST /WFPC2 data to reveal details of at least two light-echo structures, including some unknown before now, around SN 1993J in the galaxy M81. In particular, we see one partial sheet of material 81 pc in front of the SN and tilted 60^ relative to the disk plane of M81, and another 220 pc in front of the SN, roughly parallel to the disk. The inferred echoing material is consistent with the H 1 surface density detected in this region of M81's disk; however, these data imply a fragmented covering factor for the echoing structures.

M81, with an angular momentum vector inclined 59 ^ along PA 62 rot75 (such that the southwest side of the disk is closer to Earth), and SN 1993J, southwest of the nucleus of M81, would imply that the tilt of the NE260 dust sheet is roughly perpendicular to the disk of the galaxy. In comparison, the SW770 echo is inclined to within 30^ of the disk plane. The SN, with its massive progenitor contained within a dense gas cloud, is presumed to lie near the disk plane (shown in Fig. \ 2). We thus detect two dust structures, both extending more than one gas scale-height (.f. \ bro98 ) above the plane. SW770 sits a roughly constant 110 pc above the disk plane in M81, while NE260 appears to miss this plane by 40 pc and extends at least 60 pc above the disk.

sources.summary: Using HST resolved-star photometry, the paper treats M 81 as a nearby spiral with measurable RGB populations, deriving a 3.85 Mpc distance, mean metallicity 0.65, and thick-disk/halo density gradients.

quotes.quote: Our goal here is to extend the study of stellar thick disks and halos over a set of three spiral galaxies outside the Local group, M 81, NGC 55 and NGC 300. These galaxies provide contrasting environments, and an opportunity to examine the stellar populations on larger galactic scales. In particular we address the following two issues: the spatial distribution of different stellar populations in the outskirts of spiral galaxies and the metallicity gradient of these populations.

M 81 is the gravitationally dominant member of its group, consisting of about 30 types of galaxies (B o rngen et al. 1982, 1984; Karachentseva et al., 1985). The galaxy is a suitable object for this study because of its close distance, D=3.6 Mpc (Freedman et al., 2001) and small inclination, which provide an opportunity to study the geometry of the stellar structures across the disk plane. While the young stellar populations of M 81, populating mainly its spiral arms, have been previously studied in numerous investigations, little is known about the galaxy periphery.

The red giant population (RGB) identified at large galactocentric radii yields a distance of 3.85 0.08 Mpc for M 81, 2.12 0.10 Mpc for NGC 55, and 2.00 0.13 Mpc for NGC 300, and a mean stellar metallicity of 0.65 , 1.25 , and 0.87 respectively.
